<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Biotin is a common cofactor for <u>carboxylase</u> enzymes, since it can carry CO<sub>2</sub> molecules.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6948a0dfb15b78af89625708c2518e32.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Vitamin&#x20;B&lt;sub&gt;7&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x28;biotin&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[vitamin]</span> is a <i>common</i> cofactor used by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylase</u></span> enzymes, since it's capable of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>donating</i> CO<sub>2</sub>&nbsp;molecules</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Vitamin B<sub>7</sub> (biotin)</b></span> is a <i>common</i> cofactor used by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylase</u></span> enzymes, since it's capable of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>donating</i> CO<sub>2</sub>&nbsp;molecules</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Its COOH group can be decarboxylated for the release of CO<sub>2</sub>.</div><div>- Think of <b>pyruvate carboxylase</b>, <b>acetyl-CoA carboxylase</b>, <b>propionyl-CoA carboxylase</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-2722ec1c2251cb926c6557872a05e9a049848f6b.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Vitamin B<sub>7</sub> (biotin)</b></span> is a <i>common</i> cofactor used by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;carboxylase&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> enzymes, since it's capable of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;donating&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;CO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;molecules"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Vitamin B<sub>7</sub> (biotin)</b></span> is a <i>common</i> cofactor used by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylase</u></span> enzymes, since it's capable of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>donating</i> CO<sub>2</sub>&nbsp;molecules</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Its COOH group can be decarboxylated for the release of CO<sub>2</sub>.</div><div>- Think of <b>pyruvate carboxylase</b>, <b>acetyl-CoA carboxylase</b>, <b>propionyl-CoA carboxylase</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-2722ec1c2251cb926c6557872a05e9a049848f6b.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>acyl-TPP intermediate</u></span> is first <i>stabilized</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;vitamin&#x20;B&lt;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[cofactor]</span> on pyruvate dehydrogenase.<br></div>"	"<div>In the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>acyl-TPP intermediate</u></span> is first <i>stabilized</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>vitamin B<sub>1</sub></b></span> on pyruvate dehydrogenase.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Lipoic acid on dihydrolipoyl transacetylase then grabs on to acyl-TPP (stabilized by lipoic acid), and converts it into acetyl-CoA.</div><div>- Acyl-TPP is created after the first decarboxylation of pyruvate<br><br></div><img src=""b6bdc6c1ddd8edef6dc8102c9d282e7a.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">NAD<sup style="""">+</sup></span>&nbsp;is regenerated for the use of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;GA3P&amp;nbsp&#x3B;dehydrogenase&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[enzyme]</span> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>lactate dehydrogenase</b></span> in anaerobic fermentation."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">NAD<sup style="""">+</sup></span>&nbsp;is regenerated for the use of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>GA3P&nbsp;dehydrogenase</u></span> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>lactate dehydrogenase</b></span> in anaerobic fermentation.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""cb7628f7c5f145df306cbb16b71e78f1.png""></div>"
"During an enzymatic reaction, <b>enzymes</b> form the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;tighest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weakest/tightest]</span> bonds to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>transition-state complex</u></span> than either the substrate or product in order to stabilize it - facilitating catalysis."	"During an enzymatic reaction, <b>enzymes</b> form the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>tighest</i></span> bonds to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>transition-state complex</u></span> than either the substrate or product in order to stabilize it - facilitating catalysis.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is called the <b>transition-state model</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><br></div><div><img src=""6510e8a58dc771b5e87499d3c1ab3d48.png""></div></div>"
"During an enzymatic reaction, <b>enzymes</b> form the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>tighest</i></span> bonds to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;transition&#x2D;state&#x20;complex&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> than either the substrate or product in order to stabilize it - facilitating catalysis."	"During an enzymatic reaction, <b>enzymes</b> form the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>tighest</i></span> bonds to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>transition-state complex</u></span> than either the substrate or product in order to stabilize it - facilitating catalysis.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is called the <b>transition-state model</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><br></div><div><img src=""6510e8a58dc771b5e87499d3c1ab3d48.png""></div></div>"
"In triglyceride synthesis, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;acyltransferase&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[enzyme]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>attaches</i>&nbsp;<u>fatty acids</u></span>&nbsp;onto the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">glycerol backbone</span> to form triglycerides."	"In triglyceride synthesis, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>acyltransferase</b></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>attaches</i>&nbsp;<u>fatty acids</u></span>&nbsp;onto the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">glycerol backbone</span> to form triglycerides.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-b394e3b0861c4ea969168f7654dec7f03d25c666.jpg""></div>"
"In triglyceride synthesis, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>acyltransferase</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;attaches&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;fatty&#x20;acids&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;onto the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""glycerol&#x20;backbone"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> to form triglycerides." "Eukaryotes &amp; prokaryotes use <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[the same/different]</span> types of&nbsp;<b>RNA polymerases</b>&nbsp;in transcription." "Gene <b>repressors</b>&nbsp;<i>inhibit</i> the transcription of a gene by <i>inhibiting</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""RNA&#x20;polymerase&#x27;s&#x20;movement"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> along the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;promoter&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;sequence"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>." These are important in translation.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-72e2b69f5b2490521f16da1a0aa7756c9b007143.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2966e8fc7947467dce265d073b74b626.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Bohr&#x20;effect&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[effect]</span> says <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub> &amp; H<sup>+</sup></span>&nbsp;<i>inhibit(s)</i> <u>hemoglobin's affinity</u> for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span> - causing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span> <i>delivery</i> into tissues."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bohr effect</b></span> says <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub> &amp; H<sup>+</sup></span>&nbsp;<i>inhibit(s)</i> <u>hemoglobin's affinity</u> for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span> - causing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span> <i>delivery</i> into tissues.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Haldane effect</b> is similar, but it says that O<sub>2</sub> affects CO<sub>2</sub> &amp; H<sup>+</sup>'s affinity for hemoglobin - resulting in their deliver into tissues.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7c8917f4e25840737b200a9c1b8e2a26f04a5b69.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bohr effect</b></span> says <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""CO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<i>inhibit(s)</i> <u>hemoglobin's affinity</u> for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - causing <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>delivery</i> into tissues."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bohr effect</b></span> says <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub> &amp; H<sup>+</sup></span>&nbsp;<i>inhibit(s)</i> <u>hemoglobin's affinity</u> for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span> - causing <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span> <i>delivery</i> into tissues.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Haldane effect</b> is similar, but it says that O<sub>2</sub> affects CO<sub>2</sub> &amp; H<sup>+</sup>'s affinity for hemoglobin - resulting in their deliver into tissues.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7c8917f4e25840737b200a9c1b8e2a26f04a5b69.jpg""></div>"

<div>...becuase you'd be adding a new sequence of nucleotides in the middle of the previous sequence of nucleotides - disrupting its activity.</div><div><br></div><img src=""162736e622b69e1b48411bf5144f51a1.png""></div>"
"In saponification reactions, the <b>ester</b> is converted into a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;carboxylate&#x20;salt&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;COO&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;Na&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[product]</span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"To sequence <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">amino acids</span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>2,4-dinitrofluorobenzene (DNFB)</b></span> is a reagent that <i>attacks</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>N-terminus</u></span> of peptide sequences via <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">aromatic substitutions</span> to reveal their identities.

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Developed by Sanger, who <u>also</u> developed the <b>chain-terminating method</b> of <u>DNA</u> sequencing.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e4242480da53e075c08a8468c9636e15114a9773.jpg""></div>"
"Under certain conditions that allow <i style=""""><b>chiral</b></i><b> amino acids</b> to <u>tautomerize</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""enols"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are formed as intermediates - resulting in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""racemic&#x20;mixture"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> when they <i>revert back</i> to the ketone form."	"Under certain conditions that allow <i style=""""><b>chiral</b></i><b> amino acids</b> to <u>tautomerize</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">enols</span> are formed as intermediates - resulting in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">racemic mixture</span> when they <i>revert back</i> to the ketone form.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Amino acids don't spontaneously tautomerize - this will only happen under very specific conditions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-41de6ac3ca7e67c48cfe05442d6ecef21ea5d320.jpg""></div>"
"In <u>amino acid sequencing</u>, the identity of each amino acid <i>associated</i> with DNFB is analyzed using <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;sup&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;H&#x20;NMR&#x20;spectroscopy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[technique]</span>."	"In <u>amino acid sequencing</u>, the identity of each amino acid <i>associated</i> with DNFB is analyzed using <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- NMR can be useful to discriminate between minor differences in functional groups.</div><div>- <b>DNFB</b> is the reagent used in this form of sequencing. It attacks the N-terminus of the protein and breaks it off through an aromatic substitution reaction.</div><div><br></div><div>- Edman degradation: analyzes the residue with mass spectrometry.<div>- Sanger sequencing: analyzes the residue with NMR spectroscopy.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e4242480da53e075c08a8468c9636e15114a9773.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In the cell cycle, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Rb&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[protein]</span> <i>inhibits</i> the transition from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">G<sub>1</sub></span> → <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">S</span> when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>DNA is damaged</u></span> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>inhibiting</i>&nbsp;transcription of S-phase TFs</span> - which it accomplishes by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>recruiting</i> histone deacetylase</span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>Silent mutation</b>: has no effect on the overall protein's function.<div>-&nbsp;<b>Missense mutation</b>: 1 amino acid is substituted for another that does affect the protein's structure.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Nonsense mutation</b>: encodes a premature stop codon (also called truncation mutations).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c59257855a49c12b5b0f34d18987fd88.png""></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>Silent mutation</b>: has no effect on the overall protein's function (wobble position).<div>-&nbsp;<b>Missense mutation</b>: 1 amino acid is substituted for another that does affect the protein's structure.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Nonsense mutation</b>: encodes a premature stop codon (also called truncation mutations).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c59257855a49c12b5b0f34d18987fd88.png""></div></div>"
"A <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;non&#x2D;conservative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <b>point mutation</b>&nbsp;in DNA is one where the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>category</u> (acidic, basic, polar, etc.)</span> of the resulting amino acid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>is</i></span> changed."	"A <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>non-conservative</i></b></span> <b>point mutation</b>&nbsp;in DNA is one where the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>category</u> (acidic, basic, polar, etc.)</span> of the resulting amino acid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>is</i></span> changed.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>Silent mutation</b>: has no effect on the overall protein's function (wobble position).<div>-&nbsp;<b>Missense mutation</b>: 1 amino acid is substituted for another that does affect the protein's structure.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Nonsense mutation</b>: encodes a premature stop codon (also called truncation mutations).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c59257855a49c12b5b0f34d18987fd88.png""></div></div>"
"Bacterial genomes use <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;DNA&#x20;methylase&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[enzyme]</span> to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;methylate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;their&#x20;own&#x20;DNA"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> during replication - allowing them to differentiate between <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">their own DNA</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">foreign DNA (i.e. viral)</span>."	"Bacterial genomes use <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>DNA methylase</u></span> to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>methylate</i> their own DNA</span> during replication - allowing them to differentiate between <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">their own DNA</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">foreign DNA (i.e. viral)</span>.

"In Sanger Sequencing, <b>ddNTPs</b> are incorporated into the growing strand to terminate its growth, due to the fact that it <i>lacks</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""C3&#x27;&#x20;OH"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - <i>preventing</i> the formation of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""phosphodiester&#x20;bond"" data-ordinal=""1"">[group]</span>."	"In Sanger Sequencing, <b>ddNTPs</b> are incorporated into the growing strand to terminate its growth, due to the fact that it <i>lacks</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">C3' OH</span> - <i>preventing</i> the formation of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">phosphodiester bond</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Phosphodiester bonds are formed between the C3' OH of one nucleotide and the C5 phosphate group of another nucleotide.</div><div>- DNA/RNA molecules differ by the presence/absence of a C2 OH.</div><div><br></div><img src=""75c89e738b2b1123ee383f5eb1fcf141.png""></div>"
"In DNA, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;short&#x20;tandem&#x20;repeats&#x20;&#x28;STRs&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>microsattelites</u></span>." "<div>Effects on <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>- Direct inhibition of ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub> consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>.</div><div>- H<sup>+</sup> flow uncoupled from ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub> consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>.</div>"	"<div>Effects on <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>- Direct inhibition of ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub> consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span>.</div><div>- H<sup>+</sup> flow uncoupled from ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub> consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Uncoupling H<sup>+</sup> flow: H<sup>+</sup> protons constantly flow back into the mitochondrial matrix. This causes a rapid depletion of the gradient, which&nbsp;incentivizes even more H<sup>+</sup> pumping - resulting in more O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div>- <u>Inhibiting ATP synthase</u>: without an active ATP synthase, H<sup>+</sup> accumulates in the intermembrane space - creating electrostatic repulsions. Electrons would stop flowing in the ETC due to the lack of proton pumping - decreasing O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""aJqoR.gif""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Effects on <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>- H<sup>+</sup> flow uncoupled from ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub> consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>.</div><div>- Direct inhibition of ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub>&nbsp;consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>Effects on <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>- H<sup>+</sup> flow uncoupled from ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub> consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span>.</div><div>- Direct inhibition of ATP synthase: O<sub>2</sub>&nbsp;consumption <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Uncoupling H<sup>+</sup> flow: H+ protons constantly flow back into the mitochondrial matrix. This causes a rapid depletion of the gradient, which&nbsp;incentivizes even more H+ pumping - resulting in more O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div>- <u>Inhibiting ATP synthase</u>: without an active ATP synthase, H<sup>+</sup> accumulates in the intermembrane space - creating electrostatic repulsions. Electrons would stop flowing in the ETC due to the lack of proton pumping - decreasing O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""aJqoR.gif""><br></div></div>"
"<div>The direct <i>inhibition</i> of <u>ATP synthase</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sup>+</sup> accumulates</span>&nbsp;in the intermembrane space - creating repulsions that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>prevent</i> H<sup>+</sup> pumps from transferring electrons</span>.</div>"	"<div>The direct <i>inhibition</i> of <u>ATP synthase</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sup>+</sup> accumulates</span>&nbsp;in the intermembrane space - creating repulsions that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>prevent</i> H<sup>+</sup> pumps from transferring electrons</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Electrons will not be able to flow to O<sub>2</sub> if the proton pumps stop transferring electrons.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Uncoupling H</u><sup style="""">+</sup><u> flow</u>: H<sup>+</sup> protons constantly flow back into the mitochondrial matrix. This causes a rapid depletion of the gradient, which&nbsp;incentivizes even more H<sup>+</sup> pumping - resulting in more O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div>- <u>Inhibiting ATP synthase</u>: without an active ATP synthase, H<sup>+</sup> accumulates in the intermembrane space - creating electrostatic repulsions. "<div>The direct <i>inhibition</i> of <u>ATP synthase</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;accumulates"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;in the intermembrane space - creating repulsions that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;prevent&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;pumps&#x20;from&#x20;transferring&#x20;electrons"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>The direct <i>inhibition</i> of <u>ATP synthase</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sup>+</sup> accumulates</span>&nbsp;in the intermembrane space - creating repulsions that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>prevent</i> H<sup>+</sup> pumps from transferring electrons</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Electrons will not be able to flow to O<sub>2</sub> if the proton pumps stop transferring electrons.</div><div><br></div><div>- <u>Uncoupling H</u><sup style="""">+</sup><u> flow</u>: H<sup>+</sup> protons constantly flow back into the mitochondrial matrix. This causes a rapid depletion of the gradient, which&nbsp;incentivizes even more H<sup>+</sup> pumping - resulting in more O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div>- <u>Inhibiting ATP synthase</u>: without an active ATP synthase, H<sup>+</sup> accumulates in the intermembrane space - creating electrostatic repulsions. Electrons would stop flowing in the ETC due to the lack of proton pumping - decreasing O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""aJqoR.gif""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Agents that uncouple H<sup>+</sup> flow from ATP synthase <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since they <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>dissipate</i> the H<sup>+</sup> gradient</span> - causing the ETC's proton pumps to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compensate by <i>increasing</i> their activity</span>.</div>"	"<div>Agents that uncouple H<sup>+</sup> flow from ATP synthase <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since they <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>dissipate</i> the H<sup>+</sup> gradient</span> - causing the ETC's proton pumps to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compensate by <i>increasing</i> their activity</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Uncoupling H</u><sup>+</sup><u> flow</u>: H<sup>+</sup> protons constantly flow back into the mitochondrial matrix. This causes a rapid depletion of the gradient, which&nbsp;incentivizes even more H<sup>+</sup> pumping - resulting in more O<sub>2</sub> consumption.<br></div><div>- <u>Inhibiting ATP synthase</u>: without an active ATP synthase, H<sup>+</sup> accumulates in the intermembrane space - creating electrostatic repulsions. "<div>Agents that uncouple H<sup>+</sup> flow from ATP synthase <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since they <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;dissipate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;gradient"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - causing the ETC's proton pumps to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""compensate&#x20;by&#x20;&lt;i&gt;increasing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;their&#x20;activity"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>Agents that uncouple H<sup>+</sup> flow from ATP synthase <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> <b>O<sub>2</sub> consumption</b> since they <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>dissipate</i> the H<sup>+</sup> gradient</span> - causing the ETC's proton pumps to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">compensate by <i>increasing</i> their activity</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Uncoupling H</u><sup>+</sup><u> flow</u>: H<sup>+</sup> protons constantly flow back into the mitochondrial matrix. This causes a rapid depletion of the gradient, which&nbsp;incentivizes even more H<sup>+</sup> pumping - resulting in more O<sub>2</sub> consumption.<br></div><div>- <u>Inhibiting ATP synthase</u>: without an active ATP synthase, H<sup>+</sup> accumulates in the intermembrane space - creating electrostatic repulsions. Electrons would stop flowing in the ETC due to the lack of proton pumping - decreasing O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a1bb91bfe1da79e128db436b3108cdc40ee53acb.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Silent&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>mutations</b> are mutations in DNA where the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>phenotype</i> of the <u>final protein</u></span> is not changed." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>RT-PCR</b> uses reverse transcriptase to synthesize cDNA from mRNA (RT). After this, it amplifies the cDNA into many copies of DNA (PCR).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f4d8ecec648a3226fadc0b3b7515270d7c13c92d.jpg""></div>"
"In translation, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;peptidyl&#x20;transferase&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[enzyme]</span> catalyzes both the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>formation</i></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hydrolysis</i></span> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>peptide bonds</u></span> during elongation and termination, respectively."	"In translation, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>peptidyl transferase</b></span> catalyzes both the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>formation</i></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hydrolysis</i></span> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>peptide bonds</u></span> during elongation and termination, respectively.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Catalyzing the forward &amp; reverse reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c1f34b4ad2542417613bf62a95256437.jpg""></div>"
"In translation, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>peptidyl transferase</b></span> catalyzes both the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;formation&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[reaction]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;hydrolysis&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[reaction]</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;peptide&#x20;bonds&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> during elongation and termination, respectively."	"In translation, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>peptidyl transferase</b></span> catalyzes both the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>formation</i></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>hydrolysis</i></span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>peptide bonds</u></span> during elongation and termination, respectively.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Catalyzing the forward &amp; reverse reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c1f34b4ad2542417613bf62a95256437.jpg""></div>"
"In a sugar's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>linear</i></span>&nbsp;form,&nbsp;<b>reducing sugars</b>&nbsp;can be identified by the presence of a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;aldehyde&#x20;group&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> on C1."	"In a sugar's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>linear</i></span>&nbsp;form,&nbsp;<b>reducing sugars</b>&nbsp;can be identified by the presence of a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>aldehyde group</u></span> on C1.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <b>aldehyde</b> group is the group that gets oxidized into a carboxylic acid group (meaning the aldehyde is the reducing agent).</div><div><br></div><img src=""472318df250f6907db8f48c71c824a24.png""></div>"
"In a sugar's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;linear&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[linear/cyclic]</span>&nbsp;form,&nbsp;<b>reducing sugars</b>&nbsp;can be identified by the presence of a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>aldehyde group</u></span> on C1."	"In a sugar's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>linear</i></span>&nbsp;form,&nbsp;<b>reducing sugars</b>&nbsp;can be identified by the presence of a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>aldehyde group</u></span> on C1.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <b>aldehyde</b> group is the group that gets oxidized into a carboxylic acid group (meaning the aldehyde is the reducing agent).</div><div><br></div><img src=""472318df250f6907db8f48c71c824a24.png""></div>"
"In <b>Benedict's</b> or <b>Tollen's test</b>, the functional group that <i>acts</i> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing agent</span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;aldehyde&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[group]</span> in the sugar's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;linear&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cyclic/linear]</span> form - getting oxidized into a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">carboxylic acid</span> in the process."	"In <b>Benedict's</b> or <b>Tollen's test</b>, the functional group that <i>acts</i> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing agent</span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>aldehyde</u></span> in the sugar's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>linear</i></span> form - getting oxidized into a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">carboxylic acid</span> in the process.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>You can identify reducing sugars by the presence of a free OH in the cyclic form, because cyclic carbohydrates can tautomerize back to their linear forms. Once it does this, the aldehyde is oxidized.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5d10d80da5b77fa95920648a03e9fe8f30b80f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""472318df250f6907db8f48c71c824a24.png""></div>"
"In <b>Benedict's</b> or <b>Tollen's test</b>, the functional group that <i>acts</i> as the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reducing&#x20;agent"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>aldehyde</u></span> in the sugar's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>linear</i></span> form - getting oxidized into a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">carboxylic acid</span> in the process."	"In <b>Benedict's</b> or <b>Tollen's test</b>, the functional group that <i>acts</i> as the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing agent</span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>aldehyde</u></span> in the sugar's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>linear</i></span> form - getting oxidized into a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">carboxylic acid</span> in the process.

"<b>L</b>-glucose &amp; <b>D</b>-glucose are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">epimers</span><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">&nbsp;</span>&amp;<span style=""font-weight: bold;"">&nbsp;</span><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">enantiomers</span><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">&nbsp;</span>of each other - differing in configuration at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;all&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[only some/all]</span> chiral centers."	"<b>L</b>-glucose &amp; <b>D</b>-glucose are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">epimers</span><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">&nbsp;</span>&amp;<span style=""font-weight: bold;"">&nbsp;</span><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">enantiomers</span><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">&nbsp;</span>of each other - differing in configuration at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>all</i></span> chiral centers.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Even though L/D refers to the directionality of the bottom OH, they're still enantiomers since L or D dictates the chirality at every chiral center as well.</div><div>- Shown below using erythrose as an example...</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b7549dfa1043c860c806dd2fb681b75059f88793.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Mutarotation</b> can <i>only</i> happen on <u>polysaccharides</u>&nbsp;with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hemiacetal"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acetal/hemiacetal]</span> groups."	"<b>Mutarotation</b> can <i>only</i> happen on <u>polysaccharides</u>&nbsp;with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">hemiacetal</span> groups.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Acetals are already stable in their linkages - they won't mutarotate.</div><div>- <u>Subunit 4</u> is the only sugar capable of mutarotation.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b1794c012ac4c148695d6140ecae375f9c83c382.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""f2dcf2238ad3094c3020d521c8f2196f.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Protein <b>isoforms</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>structurally similar</u> proteins</span> that are produced <i>either</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[the same/different]</span> gene(s) or through <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""alternative&#x20;splicing"" data-ordinal=""3"">[process]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Protein <b>isoforms</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>structurally similar</u> proteins</span> that are produced <i>either</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>different</i></span> gene(s) or through <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">alternative splicing</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Don't start counting by threes until you find AUG/ATG.</div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""number&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;peptides"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;that could be produced using an 'n' number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;unique&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;amino&#x20;acids"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">&nbsp;<b>n! </b>('n' factorial) =&nbsp;# of different peptides</span>&nbsp;</div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">number of <i>different</i> peptides</span>&nbsp;that could be produced using an 'n' number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>unique</i> amino acids</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">&nbsp;<b>n! </b>('n' factorial) =&nbsp;# of different peptides</span>&nbsp;</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- When n = 3 unique amino acids, 6 different peptides could be produced...<div><br></div><div>n = 3... 3 x 2 x 1 = 6.</div><div><br></div><div><img alt=""What is a Factorial? | Quality Gurus"" src=""Factorials-Formula-scaled.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">number of <i>different</i> peptides</span>&nbsp;that could be produced using an 'n' number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>unique</i> amino acids</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;b&gt;n&#x21;&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x28;&#x27;n&#x27;&#x20;factorial&#x29;&#x20;&#x3D;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x23;&#x20;of&#x20;different&#x20;peptides"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;</div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">number of <i>different</i> peptides</span>&nbsp;that could be produced using an 'n' number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>unique</i> amino acids</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">&nbsp;<b>n! </b>('n' factorial) =&nbsp;# of different peptides</span>&nbsp;</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- When n = 3 unique amino acids, 6 different peptides could be produced...<div><br></div><div>n = 3... 3 x 2 x 1 = 6.</div><div><br></div><div><img alt=""What is a Factorial? | Quality Gurus"" src=""Factorials-Formula-scaled.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div><b>Rb</b> <u>prevents the transition of G<sub>1</sub> → S</u> by preventing transcription of S-phase transcription factors.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-11692d607b803d543d02f56d4c553e4082d13546.jpg""></div>"
"When <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Rb</span> is bound to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;E2F&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[protein]</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""E2F"" data-ordinal=""2"">[protein]</span> is <i>prevented</i> from entering the nucleus &amp; inducing <u>transcription of S-phase TFs</u>."	"When <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Rb</span> is bound to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>E2F</b></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">E2F</span> is <i>prevented</i> from entering the nucleus &amp; inducing <u>transcription of S-phase TFs</u>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Rb</b> <u>prevents the transition of G<sub>1</sub> → S</u> by preventing transcription of S-phase transcription factors.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-11692d607b803d543d02f56d4c553e4082d13546.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Rb</b> <i>releases</i> its hold on E2F after it gets <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""phosphorylated"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reaction]</span> - <i>enabling</i> the transcription of S-phase TFs."	"<b>Rb</b> <i>releases</i> its hold on E2F after it gets <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">phosphorylated</span> - <i>enabling</i> the transcription of S-phase TFs.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Rb</b> prevents the transition of G<sub>1</sub> → S by preventing transcription of S-phase transcription factors.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-11692d607b803d543d02f56d4c553e4082d13546.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Suicide</b></span> <b>inhibitors</b>&nbsp;are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;irreversible&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[reversible/irreversible]</span> inhibitors that form <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""covalent"" data-ordinal=""2"">[covalent/non-covalent]</span> bonds with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">enzyme's <u>active site</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Suicide</b></span> <b>inhibitors</b>&nbsp;are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>irreversible</i></span> inhibitors that form <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">covalent</span> bonds with the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">enzyme's <u>active site</u></span>.
<hr>

<div>These might undergo the first few steps of the enzymatic reaction before then binding irreversibly with the enzyme.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b2eecda95b2b012b46b0d70d1cce26a24302b97e.jpg""></div>"

"The term ""<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;entropic&#x20;penalty&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>"" refers to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> entropy associated with water's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">ordered</span> structure around <u>hydro</u><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>phobic</u></span> residues on the surface of proteins." <div><b>Entropic penalty</b> refers to water's decrease in entropy when hydrophic compounds are placed in solution.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ee59824d78dd90495ef211217d3ff8dc4764d62a.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Pyruvate decarboxylase</b></span> <i>catalyzes</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;pyruvate&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;acetaldehyde&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;CO&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">anaerobic fermentation (ethanol)</span>." "<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>miRNAs</b></span> can be either <i>somewhat</i>&nbsp;or <i>perfectly</i> complementary RNA molecules that bind<i> </i>to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mRNA transcripts</u></span> to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""silence&#x20;their&#x20;expression"" data-ordinal=""3"">[function]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>miRNAs</b></span> can be either <i>somewhat</i>&nbsp;or <i>perfectly</i> complementary RNA molecules that bind<i> </i>to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mRNA transcripts</u></span> to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">silence their expression</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>When miRNAs are somewhat complementary, they prevent translation. When they're perfectly complementary, they cleave the transcript.</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>siRNAs</b>&nbsp;destroy&nbsp;mRNAs by signalling for their destruction.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>miRNAs</b>&nbsp;destroy mRNAs by either cutting them in half or inhibiting ribosome-binding.</div><div><br></div><img src=""2966e8fc7947467dce265d073b74b626.png""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>siRNAs</b></span> are <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;perfectly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[somewhat/perfectly]</span>&nbsp;complementary RNA molecules that&nbsp;bind<i>&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mRNA transcripts</u></span> to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cleave&#x20;them&#x20;&#x28;destruction&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[function]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>siRNAs</b></span> are <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>perfectly</i></span>&nbsp;complementary RNA molecules that&nbsp;bind<i>&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mRNA transcripts</u></span> to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">cleave them (destruction)</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>siRNAs</b> destroy mRNAs by cleaving them.</div><div>- <b>miRNAs</b> destroy mRNAs by preventing translation.</div><div><br></div><img src=""2966e8fc7947467dce265d073b74b626.png""></div>"
"<div>When <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;miRNAs&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[miRNAs/siRNAs]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>somewhat</i></span> complementary to an <u>mRNA transcript</u>, they <i>silence</i> the transcript's expression by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">preventing translation</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>When <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>miRNAs</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>somewhat</i></span> complementary to an <u>mRNA transcript</u>, they <i>silence</i> the transcript's expression by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">preventing translation</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>When miRNAs are somewhat complementary, they prevent translation. When they're perfectly complementary, they cleave the transcript.</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>siRNAs</b>&nbsp;destroy&nbsp;mRNAs by signalling for their destruction.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>miRNAs</b>&nbsp;destroy mRNAs by either cutting them in half or inhibiting ribosome-binding.</div><div><br></div><img src=""2966e8fc7947467dce265d073b74b626.png""></div>"
"<div>When <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>miRNAs</b></span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;somewhat&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[somewhat/perfectly]</span> complementary to an <u>mRNA transcript</u>, they <i>silence</i> the transcript's expression by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">preventing translation</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>When <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>miRNAs</b></span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>somewhat</i></span> complementary to an <u>mRNA transcript</u>, they <i>silence</i> the transcript's expression by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">preventing translation</span>.<br></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Ketone bodies are relied on more heavily, because hyperglycemia is a failure of the cells to uptake glucose.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><span style=""font-weight: bold;"">Glycogen</span></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">starch</b></span> are <i>both</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""poly"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>saccharides made of&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u style="""">α-1,4</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u style="""">α-1,6</u></span> linkages of glucose." <script>
// Prevent original image from loading before mask
aFade = 50, qFade = 0;
var mask = document.querySelector('#io-overlay>img');
function loaded() {
    var original = document.querySelector('#io-original');
    original.style.visibility = ""visible"";
}
if (mask === null || mask.complete) {
    loaded();
} else {
    mask.addEventListener('load', loaded);
}
</script>

"<b>Recombinant viral vectors</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""DNA"" data-ordinal=""1"">[DNA/RNA]</span> viruses, because they need to be able to allow for foreign <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""DNA"" data-ordinal=""1"">[DNA/RNA]</span> to be <u>spliced in</u>." "The term ""<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>oxygen demand</b></span>""&nbsp;refers to the <i>amount</i> of O<sub>2</sub> needed to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;keep&#x20;up&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;with&#x20;ETC&#x27;s&#x20;rate&#x20;of&#x20;O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;consumption"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The term ""<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>oxygen demand</b></span>""&nbsp;refers to the <i>amount</i> of O<sub>2</sub> needed to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>keep up</i> with ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

If the ETC is being inhibited by a compound that's reducing its rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption, oxygen demand will be low.</div>"
"The term ""<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;oxygen&#x20;demand&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>""&nbsp;refers to the <i>amount</i> of O<sub>2</sub> needed to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>keep up</i> with ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption</span>."	"The term ""<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>oxygen demand</b></span>""&nbsp;refers to the <i>amount</i> of O<sub>2</sub> needed to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>keep up</i> with ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

If the ETC is being inhibited by a compound that's reducing its rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption, oxygen demand will be low.</div>"
"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[low/high]</span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span>."	"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Oxygen demand refers to the amount of O<sub>2</sub> needed to keep up with the ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div>- If the ETC is being inhibited by a compound that's reducing its rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption, oxygen demand will be low.</div>"
"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span>."	"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Oxygen demand refers to the amount of O<sub>2</sub> needed to keep up with the ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div>- If the ETC is being inhibited by a compound that's reducing its rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption, oxygen demand will be low.</div>"
"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[low/high]</span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span>."	"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Oxygen demand refers to the amount of O<sub>2</sub> needed to keep up with the ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div>- If the ETC is being inhibited by a compound that's reducing its rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption, oxygen demand will be low.</div>"
"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span>."	"If <u>O</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u> consumption</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> in the electron transport chain,&nbsp;<b>oxygen demand</b> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Oxygen demand refers to the amount of O<sub>2</sub> needed to keep up with the ETC's rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption.</div>- If the ETC is being inhibited by a compound that's reducing its rate of O<sub>2</sub> consumption, oxygen demand will be low.</div>"

"<b>Response elements</b> in DNA's promoter region are classified as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;promoters&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> because they allow for the binding of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;specific&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;transcription&#x20;factors"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Response elements</b> in DNA's promoter region are classified as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>promoters</u></span> because they allow for the binding of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>specific</i> transcription factors</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Transcription factors might be specific for estrogen amplification, cortisol amplification, etc... key point is that DNA can only bind that one particular transcription factor.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""1f90fe81ff5ba10fdcbcb2e912ffd6bb.png""></div>"
"Adding <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""only&#x20;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction results in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>linear</i></span> amplification of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">only 1</span> strand(s)."	"Adding <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">only 1</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction results in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>linear</i></span> amplification of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">only 1</span> strand(s).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 primer → Linearly amplifies only 1 strand.<br></div><div>- 2 primers → Exponentially amplifies both strands (2<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;copies made of each strand)</div><div><br></div><img src=""781e3c762f258fd7d4ef8dddd521d7ca.png""></div>"
"Adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">only 1</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction results in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;linear&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[linear/exponential]</span> amplification of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""only&#x20;1"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span> strand(s)."	"Adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">only 1</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction results in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>linear</i></span> amplification of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">only 1</span> strand(s).

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;final&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;number&#x20;of&#x20;exponentially&#x20;amplified&#x20;copies"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in <b>PCR</b> after the addition of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""2"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span> primer(s):<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>2<sup>n</sup></b> (n = number of cycles)</span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>final</i> number of exponentially amplified copies</span> in <b>PCR</b> after the addition of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">2</span> primer(s):<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>2<sup>n</sup></b> (n = number of cycles)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 primer → Linearly amplifies only 1 strand.<br></div><div>- 2 primers → Exponentially amplifies both strands (2<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;copies made of each strand).</div><div><br></div><img src=""781e3c762f258fd7d4ef8dddd521d7ca.png""></div>"
"After adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">only 1</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""4"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span>."	"After adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">only 1</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">4</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>When only 1 primer is used, the number of cycles equals the number of amplified copies (<u>linear</u> amplification).</div><div><br></div><div>- 1 primer → Linearly amplifies only 1 strand.<br></div><div>- 2 primers → Exponentially amplifies both strands (2<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;copies made of each strand).</div><div><br></div><img src=""781e3c762f258fd7d4ef8dddd521d7ca.png""></div>"
"After adding <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""only&#x20;1"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">4</span>."	"After adding <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">only 1</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">4</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>When only 1 primer is used, the number of cycles equals the number of amplified copies (<u>linear</u> amplification).</div><div><br></div><div>- 1 primer → Linearly amplifies only 1 strand.<br></div><div>- 2 primers → Exponentially amplifies both strands (2<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;copies made of each strand).</div><div><br></div><img src=""781e3c762f258fd7d4ef8dddd521d7ca.png""></div>"
"After adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">2</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""16&#x20;&#x28;2&lt;sup&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span>."	"After adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">2</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">16 (2<sup>4</sup>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>When 2 primers are added, both strands are replicated each time (hence why it's exponential).</div><div><br></div><div>- 1 primer → Linearly amplifies only 1 strand.<br></div><div>- 2 primers → Exponentially amplifies both strands (2<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;copies made of each strand).</div><div><br></div><img src=""781e3c762f258fd7d4ef8dddd521d7ca.png""></div>"
"After adding <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""2"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">16 (2<sup>4</sup>)</span>."	"After adding <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">2</span> <u>primer(s)</u> to a <b>PCR</b> reaction, after 4 cycles, the final number of amplified copies is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">16 (2<sup>4</sup>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>When 2 primers are added, both strands are replicated each time (hence why it's exponential).</div><div><br></div><div>- 1 primer → Linearly amplifies only 1 strand.<br></div><div>- 2 primers → Exponentially amplifies both strands (2<sup>n</sup>&nbsp;copies made of each strand).</div><div><br></div><img src=""781e3c762f258fd7d4ef8dddd521d7ca.png""></div>"
"<b>DNA</b> is <i>more</i> <u>compact</u> in the form of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""a&#x20;nucleosome"" data-ordinal=""1"">[heterochromatin/nucleosome]</span> than it is in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""heterochromatin"" data-ordinal=""1"">[heterochromatin/nucleosome]</span>."	"<b>DNA</b> is <i>more</i> <u>compact</u> in the form of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">a nucleosome</span> than it is in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">heterochromatin</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><br></div><div>- From least to most compact: <u>DNA helix</u> → <u>euchromatin</u> → <u>heterochromatin</u> → <u>nucleosome</u>.</div><div>- <b>Nucleosomes</b> are the most compact because they're strings of DNA tightly wound around histone proteins. <b>Heterochromatin</b> is slightly less compact because there's a small amount of ""free space"" between each of the connencting nucleosomes. Etc...</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-24954e98bc7b01637e064e5f8426e6eb82706610.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Levels of <b>DNA</b> <u>compactness</u> from <i>least</i> compact to <i>most</i> compact...</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Double&#x20;helix"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""euchromatin"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""heterochromatin"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""nucleosome"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><b><div></div></b>"	"<div>Levels of <b>DNA</b> <u>compactness</u> from <i>least</i> compact to <i>most</i> compact...</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Double helix</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">euchromatin</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">heterochromatin</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">nucleosome</span></div><b><div></div></b>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- From least to most compact: <u>DNA helix</u> → <u>euchromatin</u> → <u>heterochromatin</u> → <u>nucleosome</u>.<br></div><div>- <b>Nucleosomes</b> are the most compact because they're strings of DNA tightly wound around histone proteins. <b>Heterochromatin</b> is slightly less compact because there's a small amount of ""free space"" between each of the connencting nucleosomes. Etc...</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-24954e98bc7b01637e064e5f8426e6eb82706610.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Why is DNA in its nucleosome form considered <i>more</i> <u>compact</u> than heterochromatin?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Nucleosomes&#x20;are&#x20;formed&#x20;when&#x20;DNA&#x20;tightly&#x20;winds&#x20;around&#x20;histone&#x20;proteins&#x2E;&#x20;Heterochromatin&#x20;is&#x20;built&#x20;from&#x20;repeating&#x20;nucleosomes&#x2C;&#x20;but&#x20;they&#x27;re&#x20;held&#x20;together&#x20;through&#x20;short&#x20;&quot;free&#x20;spaces&#x2E;&quot;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Why is DNA in its nucleosome form considered <i>more</i> <u>compact</u> than heterochromatin?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Nucleosomes are formed when DNA tightly winds around histone proteins. Heterochromatin is built from repeating nucleosomes, but they're held together through short ""free spaces.""</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- From least to most compact: <u>DNA helix</u> → <u>euchromatin</u> → <u>heterochromatin</u> → <u>nucleosome</u>.<br></div><div>- These ""free spaces"" are stretches of DNA base pairs that are not would into the histone.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-24954e98bc7b01637e064e5f8426e6eb82706610.jpg""></div>"

<div><img src=""maxresdefault (17).jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Cadherins&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[CAMs]</span> <i>primarily</i> function in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cell-to-cell adhesion</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cadherins</b></span> <i>primarily</i> function in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cell-to-cell adhesion</u></span>.

<div><img src=""11255.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cadherins</b></span> <i>primarily</i> function in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;cell&#x2D;to&#x2D;cell&#x20;adhesion&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cadherins</b></span> <i>primarily</i> function in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cell-to-cell adhesion</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""11255.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"If the equilibrium between <u>glycolysis</u> &amp; <u>gluconeogenesis</u> normally <i>favors</i> glycolysis, how does gluconeogenesis ever occur?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""The&#x20;production&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;PEP&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;fructose&#x2D;6&#x2D;P&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;by&#x20;gluconeogenesis&#x2D;specific&#x20;enzymes&#x20;shifts&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;overall&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;equilibrium&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;reversible&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;enzymes&#x20;to&#x20;instead&#x20;favor&#x20;gluconeogenesis&#x20;&#x28;LeChatleir&#x27;s&#x20;principle&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"If the equilibrium between <u>glycolysis</u> &amp; <u>gluconeogenesis</u> normally <i>favors</i> glycolysis, how does gluconeogenesis ever occur?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">The production of <u>PEP</u> &amp; <u>fructose-6-P</u> by gluconeogenesis-specific enzymes shifts the <i>overall</i> equilibrium of the <i>reversible</i> enzymes to instead favor gluconeogenesis (LeChatleir's principle).</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is NOT saying that enzymes change the equilibrium ratios! This only means that the enzymes responsible for gluconeogenesis produce lots of PEP and F-6-P. It is the presence of those compounds (NOT the enzymes) that shift the equilibrium.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6948a0dfb15b78af89625708c2518e32.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Amphoteric&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> compounds are <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;amphiprotic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> compounds - these have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>both</i> <u>acidic</u> &amp; <u>basic</u></span> groups."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Amphoteric</b></span> compounds are <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>amphiprotic</b></span> compounds - these have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>both</i> <u>acidic</u> &amp; <u>basic</u></span> groups.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup></b> is amphiprotic/amphoteric, since it can either gain a proton (H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>) or lose a proton (CO<sub>3</sub> + H<sup>+</sup>).</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Amphoteric</b></span> compounds are <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>amphiprotic</b></span> compounds - these have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;both&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;acidic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;basic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> groups."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Amphoteric</b></span> compounds are <i>also</i> called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>amphiprotic</b></span> compounds - these have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>both</i> <u>acidic</u> &amp; <u>basic</u></span> groups.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup></b> is amphiprotic/amphoteric, since it can either gain a proton (H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>) or lose a proton (CO<sub>3</sub> + H<sup>+</sup>).</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;RNA&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[DNA/RNA]</span> has a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <u>melting point</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;DNA&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[DNA/RNA]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>RNA</b></span> has a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <u>melting point</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>DNA</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Due to RNA's A-form helix (more compact = more Van der Waals)</div>"
"Difference between <u>phosphorylases</u> &amp; <u>phosphatases</u>...<div><br></div><div><b>Phosphorylases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;add&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;phosphates&#x20;from&#x20;inorganic&#x20;sources&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><b>Phosphatases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;remove&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;phosphates&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Difference between <u>phosphorylases</u> &amp; <u>phosphatases</u>...<div><br></div><div><b>Phosphorylases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>add</i> phosphates from inorganic sources.</span></div><div><b>Phosphatases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>remove</i> phosphates.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Difference between <u>phosphorylases</u> &amp; <u>phosphatases</u>...<div><br></div><div><b>Phosphatases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;remove&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;phosphates&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><b>Phosphorylases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;add&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;phosphates&#x20;from&#x20;inorganic&#x20;sources&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Difference between <u>phosphorylases</u> &amp; <u>phosphatases</u>...<div><br></div><div><b>Phosphatases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>remove</i> phosphates.</span></div><div><b>Phosphorylases</b>: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>add</i>&nbsp;phosphates from inorganic sources.</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>In order for a point mutation to be visualized with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Southern&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>blot</b>, the mutation must have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>disrupted</i> a <u>palindromic sequence</u></span> - revealing that the mutated band was <i>unable</i> to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cleaved by a restriction enzyme</span>.</div>"	"<div>In order for a point mutation to be visualized with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Southern</b></span> <b>blot</b>, the mutation must have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>disrupted</i> a <u>palindromic sequence</u></span> - revealing that the mutated band was <i>unable</i> to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cleaved by a restriction enzyme</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EUcfS1t3iEM"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EUcfS1t3iEM</a><br></div><div>- When DNA is treated with a restriction enzyme, the DNA sequence is split into multiple bands. If a point mutation occurs in the restriction region of the DNA, restriction enzymes will be&nbsp;<i>unable</i>&nbsp;to cleave it. Once analyzed by the Southern blot, the mutated DNA will show as 1 band (since it wasn't able to be cut), while a normal version of that DNA will show as multiple bands (since it got cut).</div><div>- Ex: in the normal hemoglobin gene, the sequence can be cut my MsII. When the sequence has a point mutation in the restriction region, the restriction enzyme is unable to make a cut. Once analyzed with a Southern blot, only 1 band shows instead of the normal 2.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-61c6ef82cdae384865fef5b80e887dc5e01f0522.jpg""><br></div>"
"<div>In order for a point mutation to be visualized with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Southern</b></span> <b>blot</b>, the mutation must have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;disrupted&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;u&gt;palindromic&#x20;sequence&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - revealing that the mutated band was <i>unable</i> to be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cleaved&#x20;by&#x20;a&#x20;restriction&#x20;enzyme"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In order for a point mutation to be visualized with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Southern</b></span> <b>blot</b>, the mutation must have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>disrupted</i> a <u>palindromic sequence</u></span> - revealing that the mutated band was <i>unable</i> to be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">cleaved by a restriction enzyme</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EUcfS1t3iEM"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EUcfS1t3iEM</a><br></div><div>- When DNA is treated with a restriction enzyme, the DNA sequence is split into multiple bands. If a point mutation occurs in the restriction region of the DNA, restriction enzymes will be&nbsp;<i>unable</i>&nbsp;to cleave it. Once analyzed by the Southern blot, the mutated DNA will show as 1 band (since it wasn't able to be cut), while a normal version of that DNA will show as multiple bands (since it got cut).</div><div>- Ex: in the normal hemoglobin gene, the sequence can be cut my MsII. When the sequence has a point mutation in the restriction region, the restriction enzyme is unable to make a cut. Once analyzed with a Southern blot, only 1 band shows instead of the normal 2.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-61c6ef82cdae384865fef5b80e887dc5e01f0522.jpg""><br></div>"
"<div>Explain how <b>Southern blots</b> can be used to detect <u>point mutations</u> in DNA.<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""When&#x20;DNA&#x20;is&#x20;treated&#x20;with&#x20;a&#x20;restriction&#x20;enzyme&#x2C;&#x20;the&#x20;DNA&#x20;sequence&#x20;is&#x20;split&#x20;into&#x20;multiple&#x20;bands&#x2E;&#x20;If&#x20;a&#x20;point&#x20;mutation&#x20;occurs&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;restriction&#x20;region&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;DNA&#x2C;&#x20;restriction&#x20;enzymes&#x20;will&#x20;be&#x20;&lt;i&gt;unable&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;cleave&#x20;it&#x2E;&#x20;Once&#x20;analyzed&#x20;by&#x20;the&#x20;Southern&#x20;blot&#x2C;&#x20;the&#x20;mutated&#x20;DNA&#x20;will&#x20;show&#x20;as&#x20;1&#x20;band&#x20;&#x28;since&#x20;it&#x20;wasn&#x27;t&#x20;able&#x20;to&#x20;be&#x20;cut&#x29;&#x2C;&#x20;while&#x20;a&#x20;normal&#x20;version&#x20;of&#x20;that&#x20;DNA&#x20;will&#x20;show&#x20;as&#x20;multiple&#x20;bands&#x20;&#x28;since&#x20;it&#x20;got&#x20;cut&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Explain how <b>Southern blots</b> can be used to detect <u>point mutations</u> in DNA.<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">When DNA is treated with a restriction enzyme, the DNA sequence is split into multiple bands. If a point mutation occurs in the restriction region of the DNA, restriction enzymes will be <i>unable</i> to cleave it. Once analyzed by the Southern blot, the mutated DNA will show as 1 band (since it wasn't able to be cut), while a normal version of that DNA will show as multiple bands (since it got cut).</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This means that in order for a point mutation to be visualized with a Southern blot, the mutation must have occurred within a restriction site.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EUcfS1t3iEM"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EUcfS1t3iEM</a></div><div>- Ex: in the normal hemoglobin gene, the sequence can be cut my MsII. When the sequence has a point mutation in the restriction region, the restriction enzyme is unable to make a cut. Once analyzed with a Southern blot, only 1 band shows instead of the normal 2.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-61c6ef82cdae384865fef5b80e887dc5e01f0522.jpg""><br></div>"
"When designing an experiment to test the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>mechanism</i> of an enzyme inhibitor</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&#x5B;E&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> should <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain <i>constant</i></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&#x5B;S&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> should <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>vary</i></span>, and the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">presence or absence</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;inhibitor&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> should vary."	"When designing an experiment to test the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>mechanism</i> of an enzyme inhibitor</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>[E]</b></span> should <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">remain <i>constant</i></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>[S]</b></span> should <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>vary</i></span>, and the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">presence or absence</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>inhibitor</b></span> should vary.
